6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

In July 2025, Python hit 26.98% on the TIOBE index. To put that in perspective, no language had commanded that kind of share in the modern era of the index. Python wasn’t just the most popular language. It was lapping the field.
Eleven months later, in the June 2026 index, Python sits at 18.96%.
That’s roughly 8 percentage points gone in under a year, and the June reading marks the first time since the peak that Python has dropped below 19%. It’s still comfortably number one, still nearly 8 points ahead of second place C. Nobody is dethroning it this year or probably this decade. But an 8 point slide from an all time high is not noise, and the interesting question isn’t “is Python dying” (it isn’t), it’s “where did those points go?”
Because popularity share is a zero sum game. If Python gave up territory, somebody took it.
Let’s follow the map.
First, a necessary disclaimer about what we’re measuring
TIOBE doesn’t measure lines of code, downloads, or jobs. It measures search engine visibility: how often people are searching for a language across Google, Bing, Wikipedia, Amazon and a couple dozen other sites. It’s an attention index.
